The 1951 Agreement between Denmark and the United States concerning the Defense of Greenland — officially called: “Agreement pursuant to the North Atlantic Treaty concerning the Defense of Greenland” — signed in Copenhagen on 27 April 1951 between the Kingdom of Denmark and the United States of America. 📜 What is the Agreement? • The Agreement was concluded as a bilateral supplement to NATO cooperation and aims to ensure defense and common security in the North Atlantic, including Greenland. • It stipulates that the United States shall assist Denmark in the defense of Greenland and grants American forces the right to free access and movement to defense areas in Greenland — by land, sea and air — in cooperation with Denmark. 🗓️Key points about the agreement • Date of signing: April 27, 1951. • Background: After World War II, NATO was established, and Denmark wanted greater control and influence over the defense of Greenland (which was previously based on an agreement from 1941). This led to the new defense agreement, which integrated the cooperation into the NATO framework. • Content: The agreement ensures that the United States and Denmark cooperate on the establishment and operation of defense installations in Greenland by mutual agreement and in accordance with NATO plans. • Significance: The agreement forms the basis for the American military presence in Greenland — including Thule Air Base (now Pituffik Space Base) — which remains strategically important for the United States and NATO. In short, it is the central Danish-American defense and security agreement on Greenland from 1951 that has shaped cooperation on Arctic security since the Cold War.
